children.value: After this lecture, students should be able to:
State why the seismic moment tensor
Classify common source processes (faulting, hydrofracture, caldera collapse, landslide) by their
Read the key fields in a

— what kind of internal deformation generated the seismic waves? This is one of the most powerful tools in observational seismology: from a handful of numbers we can infer fault orientation, slip direction, and even whether the source involves volume change (explosions, collapse, opening cracks).
The moment tensor framework connects naturally to:
(Modules 3–5): the MT enters the forward problem as a source term whose radiation pattern modulates observed amplitudes.
(Module 6): accurate locations are a prerequisite for moment tensor inversion, and MT inversion in turn refines source depth.

This is the key insight: diagonalization
(eigenvectors): relates to fault strike, dip, slip direction
(eigenvalue ratios): tells us whether the source is pure shear, explosive, or something more complex
Two moment tensors can have identical eigenvalues (same source type) but different eigenvectors (different fault orientations), or vice versa.

of poor data coverage or an incorrect velocity model, not real physics.

children.value: Moment tensor inversion methods form a
ladder of increasing data requirements and model dependence
Use P-wave first motions (up = compression, down = dilatation) at many stations
(strike, dip, rake) to the polarity pattern
DC only — cannot detect ISO or CLVD components
Use amplitude ratios (e.g., P/S, SV/SH) in addition to polarity
Adds constraints on mechanism, can partially resolve non-DC components
Fit long-period waveforms (periods > 50 s) using 1-D Earth models
Fit full waveforms at regional distances (< 1000 km)
Can resolve smaller events and non-DC components
Fit waveforms at all frequencies using 3-D Earth models
Active area of research; computationally expensive

children.value: You observe that all P-wave first motions from an earthquake are compressional (up). What can you say about the moment tensor? Is this consistent with a pure DC?
A catalog reports an earthquake with 90% DC, 5% CLVD, 5% ISO. Is the CLVD component necessarily “real” physics? What factors could produce spurious non-DC content?
Why can’t far-field seismic data alone distinguish between the fault plane and the auxiliary plane of a double couple? What additional information would you need?

moment tensors from scratch and verify symmetry and eigenproperties
the ISO/DEV and DC/CLVD decomposition algorithm step by step

Takeoff angles determine which part of the radiation pattern each station samples.
Long-period surface waves are the primary data for global MT inversion.
Accurate locations are a prerequisite for MT inversion; location errors propagate into mechanism errors.
